title: Extreme Environments: reading informational text; Extreme Weather around the World
description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using informational text
topic_path: Extreme Environments: reading informational text; Extreme Weather around the World

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the practice question. 

___

##Extreme Weather around the World##

1. Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on. It seems that we only get a few days a year when the weather is “just right”. But really, all of these complaints seem silly when thinking about the world’s extreme climates. 

![A map of the world highlighting the location of the Atacama Desert in Chile, Mawsynram in India, Dallol in Ethiopia, and Oymyakon in Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/24303be9f6f7c4ca6f16909cb58fb379.jpg)   
*Map of the world*

###Extremely Hot###   

2. Is the harsh, scorching heat making your sandals sweaty? Well, in Dallol, Ethiopia, your shoes could melt if you stand still for a few minutes. Dallol is officially the hottest inhabited place on Earth. It’s part of a desert region that is below sea level. In addition, Dallol sits on top of a volcano. The intense heat [[☃ definition 1]] most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind. The Afar people of Dallol harvest this salt to sell at local markets.

![The salt slabs of Dallol, Ethiopia, which are green and red in color.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e249d358ad54a6901660db7499a42f3.png)
*Picture 1*

###Extremely Cold###  

3. You don’t know cold unless you’ve been to Oymyakon, Russia. This village is located far to the north in an area called the Sakha Republic. It has the honor of being one of the coldest places in the world where people live year-round. Yet, the word “cold” barely describes it. Try “freeze your eyeballs frigid”. Oymyakon’s temperatures dip far below freezing. Its dwellers have to wear goggles to protect their eyes from very strong winds and severely cold temperatures. The land is mostly inhospitable to farming crops. So the locals eat mainly meat and canned foods brought in from other towns. 

![A snowy street with brightly colored buildings in the small town of Oymyakon, Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6afbe8647f2a675b2a36b2131492d8f6.jpg)
*Picture 2*

###Extremely Dry###

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

**Which sentence best summarizes the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: when a liquid turns to vapor or gas

options.explanation:
Estimated 900L

*Image source: [“Piedras Rojas, Atacama Desert, Chile”](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Piedras_Rojas,_Atacama_Desert,_Chile.jpg) by Wescottm is licensed under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/deed.en).*

*Image source: [“Oymyakon - 190228 DSC 5642”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oymyakon_-_190228_DSC_5642.jpg) by Ilya Varlamov is licensed under [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en).*

*Image source: [“The hydrothermal system of Dallol, Ethiopia”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_hydrothermal_system_of_Dallol,_Ethiopia.png) by Electra Kotopoulou is licensed under [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en).*

choices.content: Around the world, people have found various ways to adapt to extreme weather conditions.
People from desert areas have to be creative to find sources of water to drink and farm with.
Every part of the world has its own, special climate, with its own average rainfall and temperature.
An area’s climate is a big factor in the health and happiness of the people who live there.

Read the passage, then answer the practice question.

People who live in the world's extreme climates have found creative ways to adapt.
Chile, Russia, Ethiopia, and India are examples of places with intense environments.
It’s easier to appreciate your own climate when comparing it to places around the world.

choices.content: Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on.

Around the world, some places have very extreme climates. Some of these places are Chile, Russia, Ethiopia, and India. People in these places have come up with smart ways to deal with the extreme weather.
Chile's Atacama desert doesn't get very much rain. Some areas haven't seen rain in 400 years. People who live there collect the water from the fog. They call this "harvesting clouds."
If you live in a place that has nice weather, you should be grateful. Many people live in places that have extreme climates. It's hard to live somewhere where it's too hot, cold, wet, or dry.

##Practice Questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

**Why do people in the Atacama desert “harvest” clouds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**Which sentence from the article supports the answer to Part A?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.definition: when a liquid turns to vapor or gas

Estimated 900L

choices.content: It rarely rains there, so they collect water from the fog in the air.
They want to shade themselves from the harsh sun.
It rains there almost every day, so they study ways to slow precipitation.
They want to find a way to stop pollution in their community.
“When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.” (paragraph 4)
“They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain.” (paragraph 5)
“Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it.” (paragraph 4)
“The intense heat evaporates most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind.” (paragraph 2)

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the practice question. 

___

##Extreme Weather around the World##

1. Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on. It seems that we only get a few days a year when the weather is “just right”. But really, all of these complaints seem silly when thinking about the world’s extreme climates. 

![A map of the world highlighting the location of the Atacama Desert in Chile, Mawsynram in India, Dallol in Ethiopia, and Oymyakon in Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/24303be9f6f7c4ca6f16909cb58fb379.jpg)   
*Map of the world*

###Extremely Hot###   

2. Is the harsh, scorching heat making your sandals sweaty? Well, in Dallol, Ethiopia, your shoes could melt if you stand still for a few minutes. Dallol is officially the hottest inhabited place on Earth. It’s part of a desert region that is below sea level. In addition, Dallol sits on top of a volcano. The intense heat [[☃ definition 1]] most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind. The Afar people of Dallol harvest this salt to sell at local markets.

![The salt slabs of Dallol, Ethiopia, which are green and red in color.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e249d358ad54a6901660db7499a42f3.png)
*Picture 1*

###Extremely Cold###  

3. You don’t know cold unless you’ve been to Oymyakon, Russia. This village is located far to the north in an area called the Sakha Republic. It has the honor of being one of the coldest places in the world where people live year-round. Yet, the word “cold” barely describes it. Try “freeze your eyeballs frigid”. Oymyakon’s temperatures dip far below freezing. Its dwellers have to wear goggles to protect their eyes from very strong winds and severely cold temperatures. The land is mostly inhospitable to farming crops. So the locals eat mainly meat and canned foods brought in from other towns. 

![A snowy street with brightly colored buildings in the small town of Oymyakon, Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6afbe8647f2a675b2a36b2131492d8f6.jpg)
*Picture 2*

###Extremely Dry###

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

**How does the sentence connect to the ideas in the text?**

*Image source: [“Oymyakon - 190228 DSC 5642”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oymyakon_-_190228_DSC_5642.jpg) by Ilya Varlamov is licensed under [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en).*

choices.content: It shows how people can deal with extreme climates.
It shows that clothing choices are less important in certain places.
It shows how a weather forecaster’s outfit should be carefully chosen each day.
It shows that umbrellas are not the only way to protect yourself from heavy rainfall.

choices.content: The ideas contrast because paragraph 4 is about not having enough rain, and paragraph 5 is about having too much rain.
The ideas are connected because paragraph 4 describes what causes too little rain, and paragraph 5 describes how to gather water.
The ideas contrast because paragraph 4 is about the advantages of living in the desert, and paragraph 5 is about the advantages of living in monsoon regions.
The ideas are connected because paragraph 5 describes the challenges of farming that are introduced in paragraph 4.

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

**How does the map help you understand the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It helps readers see where the countries in the passage are located.
It shows the locations of the world’s oceans.
It helps readers understand different weather patterns around the world.
It highlights the effects of climate change.
